does: This paper introduces an interface that exposes neural mechanisms of language models to help users anticipate behavioral outcomes when designing personalized chatbots. The core method extracts behavioral trait vectors—quantitative representations of properties like empathy, toxicity, and sycophancy—by computing differences in neural activations between contrastive system prompts that elicit opposing behaviors. To predict a chatbot's actual behaviors, the approach projects the system prompt's final token activations onto these trait vectors, normalizes the results for cross-trait comparison, and visualizes predictions through an interactive sunburst diagram. The work validates this neural transparency mechanism through an online user study comparing the proposed interface against baseline chatbot design tools.
connects: This work builds on emerging research in mechanistic interpretability that seeks to understand and expose the internal computations of neural networks. It combines activation-based interpretation methods with the practical challenge of LLM customization—an increasingly common user behavior as general-purpose language models become personalized tools for individual and organizational needs. The research responds to the opacity problem identified in prior work on emergent and unintended behaviors in prompted language models, extending earlier work on steering and probing neural activations to a concrete design interface. It also connects to growing concerns about behavioral alignment and undesirable emergent traits like sycophancy and toxicity in deployed models, addressing the gap between user intentions and actual system behavior.
enables: This interface opens pathways for more direct user control over language model deployment characteristics without requiring deep ML expertise. The neural transparency approach could enable rapid iteration on chatbot designs by providing real-time behavioral predictions before deployment, reducing the costly trial-and-error cycle. Future work might extend trait vector extraction to additional behavioral dimensions, automate the discovery of relevant traits from unlabeled behavioral data, or integrate this transparency mechanism into broader AI system design workflows. The mechanistic basis also supports potential automated safety checks—systems that flag dangerous trait combinations or predict harmful behaviors before user interaction. More broadly, this work suggests that making AI internals legible to end-users, rather than keeping them opaque, could improve both utility and safety in personalized AI systems.
